does: This paper introduces Feature Rivalry—pairs of negatively correlated features in Sparse Autoencoders—as a mechanistic signature of uncertainty in language models. The authors study Gemma-2-2B using Gemma Scope SAEs, finding that high-entropy questions produce significantly stronger feature rivalry at specific layers compared to low-entropy questions. They localize this rivalry effect to layers 0 and 12 in the residual stream through within-domain experiments on PopQA dataset. Using activation steering along rivalry axes, they demonstrate that steering along the rivalry direction causes more substantial output changes than random directions for 15 of 20 rival feature pairs, providing evidence that rivalry is causally upstream of model outputs. The authors develop a per-prompt rivalry score from pairwise SAE feature cosine similarities, establishing rivalry as a quantifiable mechanistic phenomenon underlying model uncertainty.
connects: This work builds on the emerging literature on Sparse Autoencoders for interpreting neural network representations, extending beyond prior research that has focused on identifying individual interpretable features. It connects to mechanistic interpretability research studying how uncertainty manifests in model computations, and relates to work on feature interactions in deep learning. The paper responds to the question of what internal representations look like when models are uncertain, drawing on prior entropy-based approaches to measuring confidence while grounding analysis in actual learned feature geometry. It combines insights from causal interpretation methods through activation steering with feature-level analysis, building on techniques that probe how steering along learned directions affects outputs.
enables: This framework enables future work to use feature rivalry as a diagnostic tool for detecting and characterizing model uncertainty without explicit confidence annotations. It opens mechanistic pathways for understanding how models represent conflicting information or ambiguity during reasoning. The approach could be extended to study other aspects of model computation by identifying other types of feature interactions beyond negative correlation. Results suggest applications in developing uncertainty quantification methods grounded in learned representations rather than post-hoc calibration. The per-prompt rivalry score might enable selective prompting strategies or intervention techniques that target high-uncertainty reasoning steps. Future work could investigate whether manipulating rivalry relationships influences model calibration, explores rivalry patterns across different model architectures and sizes, or applies these insights to improve model robustness and interpretability.
